title: Yorkshire puddings
subtitle: Tall, crisp, hollow puddings for the Sunday roast.
excerpt: The proper Yorkshire pudding: a thin batter of plain flour, milk, egg, and salt poured into smoking-hot beef dripping. Tall, crisp on the outside, hollow in the middle. Twelve puddings from one bowl of batter.
sourceNotes: Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, public domain), gives the first recorded recipe for a "dripping pudding" — the direct ancestor of the Yorkshire. Mrs Beeton (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) carries the form forward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cooked Yorkshires freeze well. Cool fully, freeze on a tray, then bag. Reheat from frozen in a 200°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter benefits from at least an hour in the fridge before baking, and works overnight. The puddings are best straight from the oven; bake at the moment the roast comes out to rest.

content.text: Three things make Yorkshire puddings rise tall. The oven goes screaming hot — 230°C, no lower. The fat in the tin is smoking before the batter goes in. And the batter has rested for an hour so the flour is fully hydrated and the bubbles in the egg structure have settled out.

Equal volumes of egg, milk, and flour is the canonical ratio. Measure the eggs in the jug first, then match flour and milk to that volume. Salt is generous. Don't open the oven door for the first 18 minutes; the puddings collapse on cold air.

A proper 12-hole Yorkshire pudding tin is what you want; a 12-hole muffin tin is the closest substitute. The deeper wells of a Yorkshire tin give the proper height; a four-hole tin gives bigger but fewer puddings.

Crack {{eggs}} of eggs into a measuring jug and note the volume; it should be around 250 ml. Tip into a large bowl with {{plain-flour}} of flour, {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Whisk hard for 1 minute until smooth and bubbly. Cover and refrigerate at least 1 hour, or overnight.

Heat the oven to 230°C with the empty Yorkshire tin inside. After 15 minutes, pull the tin out. Spoon {{beef-dripping}} of dripping evenly into the 12 holes — around 5 g per hole. Return to the oven for 5 minutes; the fat needs to be smoking when you pour in the batter.

Take the batter out of the fridge and stir it gently. Pull the tin out of the oven onto a heatproof surface; work fast. Pour the batter directly from a jug into each hole, filling each one half-full. The batter should hiss the moment it hits the fat. Return to the oven and shut the door.

Bake for 18 to 22 minutes. Don't open the door for the first 18 minutes. The puddings are done when they are tall, deep gold, and have risen up dramatically into curved-edged towers. Eat straight from the oven with roast beef, gravy, and horseradish cream.

items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or fat not smoking when batter went in.
items.fix: The oven must be 230°C, and the fat in the tin must be hot enough to smoke. If the batter doesn't hiss when poured, the fat is too cool.
Keep the door shut for the first 18 minutes. Cold air at the rising stage makes the structure collapse.

items.cause: Not enough fat in the holes, or non-stick tin worn through.
items.fix: Use a generous 5 g of dripping per hole. The fat is both the rising agent and the release agent.

items.cause: Batter not rested, or flour underwhisked.
items.fix: Rest the batter at least an hour. Whisk vigorously for a full minute before resting so the gluten relaxes during the rest.

content.text: Yorkshire puddings sit on the Sunday roast plate next to the beef, the potatoes, the carrots and parsnips, and the gravy. Hannah Glasse's 1747 "dripping pudding" was the ancestor: a single great pudding baked under the joint to catch the drippings. The individual rising puddings came later, codified in Mrs Beeton and on every Yorkshire roast since.
